Smartizen: Bridging the Gap in Early Childhood Education

A dual-sided platform connecting parents, facilitators, and administrators in a seamless learning ecosystem

Role UI/UX Designer
Timeline 2 Months
Platform iOS, Android
Focus Parent & Facilitator Apps
Project Overview

Transforming early childhood education through transparency and efficiency

Smartizen is a learning ecosystem designed for early childhood development programs (Whole-brain & French). I designed two mobile applications: one for parents to track their child's developmental milestones, and another for facilitators to efficiently log activities and update progress without sacrificing teaching time.

70%
Reduction in admin time
3
Taps to log activity
85%
Parent engagement increase
2
User roles designed
The Challenge

Two distinct problems, one integrated solution

Information Asymmetry

Parents lacked real-time visibility into their child's daily activities and developmental progress. Traditional methods like weekly newsletters and parent-teacher conferences created communication gaps, leaving parents unsure about milestone achievements.

Administrative Burden

Facilitators spent 40% of their time on paperwork—logging activities, tracking milestones, and communicating with parents. This "desk time" reduced actual teaching hours and led to burnout and delayed updates to parents.

The Core Question

"How might we give parents transparency into their child's learning journey while simultaneously reducing the administrative burden on facilitators?"

Research & Discovery

Understanding the stakeholders

I conducted stakeholder interviews with 8 facilitators and 15 parents to understand pain points, workflow bottlenecks, and desired outcomes.

Parent Insights

  • "I only find out about Emma's progress during quarterly meetings—I want daily updates"
  • "I have two kids in different programs. Switching between them is confusing"
  • "I can't tell if my child is actually hitting milestones or just attending class"
  • "The weekly newsletter feels generic—I want personalized updates"

Facilitator Insights

  • "Logging activities for 10 kids takes 45 minutes after each class"
  • "Parents ask the same questions repeatedly via different channels"
  • "I have to manually map each activity to curriculum milestones"
  • "Bulk actions would save hours—one photo can apply to multiple students"
Information Architecture

The three-role data ecosystem

I designed a hierarchical curriculum structure that flows from Admin → Facilitator → Parent. This architecture ensures data consistency while allowing curriculum updates without breaking historical records.

Version-Controlled Structure

I designed the milestone system to be version-controlled. When curriculum updates occur, new versions are created rather than overwriting existing data. This ensures:

  • Historical student progress remains intact
  • Parents see accurate milestone completion
  • Facilitators can reference past curriculum
  • Admin can iterate without breaking the system

Hierarchical Tagging

Every activity logged by a facilitator must be tagged with:

  • Program: Whole-brain or French
  • Category: Learning Power, Critical Thinking, Character (for Whole-brain)
  • Milestone: Specific skill (e.g., "Number Recognition 1-10")

This structure ensures parents see granular progress while facilitators benefit from auto-categorization.

User Journeys

Mapping the critical paths

I mapped end-to-end user journeys for both personas to identify friction points and optimize for the happy path.

Facilitator: Post-Class Activity Logging Flow

Parent: Daily Check-In Flow

Facilitator Flow Optimization

The legacy system required facilitators to:

  • Log into separate admin portal on desktop
  • Upload photos one by one
  • Manually write descriptions for each student
  • Search for milestone in dropdown (200+ options)
  • Repeat for every student in class

My redesign reduced this from 12 steps per student to 5 steps for an entire class.

Parent Flow Simplification

The previous communication model relied on:

  • Weekly email newsletters (generic, delayed)
  • Quarterly parent-teacher conferences
  • Ad-hoc SMS from facilitators
  • Printed milestone checklists

My redesign consolidated everything into a single daily check-in: Moments → Growth → Done.

Design Solutions

Four strategic interventions

I solved the core problems through four interconnected features—each addressing specific user pain points.

Solution 1 — Parent Experience

Global Child Context

Problem: Parents with multiple children had to log out and back in to switch context.
Solution: Persistent child selector at screen top — 1 tap to switch, no re-authentication.
Impact: 3x increase in multi-child check-ins.

Child selector pills — instant context switch

Activity feed auto-filters to selected child

Progress dashboard shows context-specific milestones

Solution 2 — Parent Transparency

Visual "Moments" Feed

Problem: Parents wanted visual proof of learning, not generic newsletters.
Solution: Chronological feed with media, descriptions, timestamps, and milestone tags.
Impact: 85% increase in daily parent engagement.

Media-rich activity updates

Class details with facilitator info

Gamified engagement — badges for parents

Solution 3 — Milestone Visibility

Growth Dashboard

Problem: Milestone progress felt abstract — parents couldn't visualize development.
Solution: Circular completion indicators (73%), progress bars (Math: 75%), expandable milestone lists.
Impact: 92% parent satisfaction for milestone visibility.

Overall progress with category breakdown

Individual student progress summary

Calendar with class scheduling

Solution 4 — Facilitator Efficiency

Bulk Activity Logging

Problem: 45 minutes post-class logging activities one-by-one for 8-10 students.
Solution: Multi-student tagging — one photo applies to entire class roster.
Impact: 70% time reduction (45 min → 12 min).

Dashboard with quick actions

Bulk upload — "Entire Class" option

Milestone tracking with status toggles

Facilitator Experience

Designing for speed and teaching-first workflows

The facilitator app needed to feel invisible—capturing learning moments without interrupting teaching.

Dashboard: quick actions + pending tasks

Class roster with attendance status

Individual student progress tracking

Bulk upload with "Entire Class" option

Milestone status: Not Started → In Progress → Completed

Parent messaging with broadcast option

Smart Defaults

  • Class auto-selected from context
  • Time-based suggestions (upload appears post-class)
  • Recent milestone memory (last-used at top)
  • "Mark All Present" bulk action

Progressive Disclosure

  • Level 1: Program tabs (Whole-brain / French)
  • Level 2: Category accordion
  • Level 3: Milestone checkboxes
  • 200+ milestones → digestible chunks

Impact

Measurable outcomes

Post-launch metrics from the pilot program with 150 families and 12 facilitators.

70%
Reduction in facilitator admin time (45 min → 12 min per class)
85%
Increase in parent engagement (daily app opens)
3 taps
Average taps to log activity (down from 12 in legacy system)
92%
Parent satisfaction score for milestone visibility

Qualitative Feedback

"I used to spend more time documenting than teaching. Now I can upload a single photo and tag all my students in one go. It's a game-changer." — Ms. Johnson, Facilitator

"I finally understand what Emma is learning. The Growth dashboard shows exactly where she's excelling and where she needs support." — Sarah Chen, Parent

Reflection

What I learned

Designing for Complex Multi-Role Ecosystems

This project taught me that designing for multi-sided platforms requires deep empathy for each role's constraints. The facilitator's need for speed directly conflicted with the parent's need for detail. I learned to resolve these tensions through strategic defaults—bulk tagging for facilitators, while ensuring parents still saw individualized updates.

The most challenging aspect was maintaining data integrity across role boundaries. I had to think in systems, not just screens. The version-controlled milestone architecture was my solution to the "moving target" problem—how do you update a curriculum without invalidating historical student progress?

If I were to iterate further, I'd explore AI-powered activity suggestions for facilitators based on milestone gaps. For example, if 60% of students haven't completed "Number Recognition 11-20," the system could recommend targeted activities. This would close the loop from parent visibility → facilitator action → student progress.

Ultimately, this project reinforced my belief that great UX isn't about flashy interactions—it's about removing friction at the exact moment it hurts most. For facilitators, that moment is the 45-minute post-class admin session. For parents, it's the quarterly meeting where they learn their child fell behind three months ago. I designed for those moments.

Designed and developed as a real-world project over 2 months

UI/UX Design • Mobile Apps • Educational Technology
